text:
Nearly a decade ago, we showed that based on nutrient content claims and the 100 gram or 100 Kcal reference amounts for a variety of food categories selecting “healthy” foods and foods “low in something” can be misleading. Now based on data for 8,596 foods in 29 food components and 25 food categories, we suggest that current FDA and Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) standards for determining whether a nutrient content claim may be made and the reference amounts of 100g or 100 Kcal can actually lead to ill health for those trying to adhere to dietary guidelines, and may contribute to the growing number of diet-related chronic diseases afflicting the developed world.

When designing foods to meet the positive nutrient requirements of the FDA and CAC regulations, the amount of calories required to meet these standards are typically greater than recommended daily allowances and often are in excess of generally accepted daily intake levels. In addition, certain nutrient-dense foods which do not qualify for the positive claims required by the CAC regulations or provided an option under the FDA program contain levels of sodium, cholesterol, and saturated fat in excess of the CAC FDA safety levels. Thus, a more satisfactory procedure has been sought which utilizes the RACCs in conjunction with energy density and maximum recommended daily dietary intake values to obtain a more meaningful and reliable measure of nutritional quality and, consequently, a revised score which reflected a marked change in the positioning of certain products; e. g. fast foods scored well on positive nutrients in accordance with the requirements of the CAC and FDA regulations because of their large RACC sizes, but scored low in relation to calorie intake as required in the proposed procedure. Hence, a new tool is available for use in providing meaningful and suitable information in the diet planning process, so that it is possible to select adequate nutrients and avoid adverse calorie intake of foods for consumption.
explanation: The passage is highly technical and reads like an academic abstract, with precise figures and terminology that can be produced by either humans or AI. The polished, formulaic style and consistent terminology slightly tilt toward AI authorship, though a real research article could also contain such language.
